Query: What is the EU Deforestation Regulation and how does it affect German timber exporters?
Response:
The EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR) is a legislative framework aimed at preventing deforestation and forest degradation associated with the import of certain commodities into the European Union. It seeks to ensure that products sold in the EU do not contribute to deforestation, thereby promoting sustainable forestry practices.

For German timber exporters, the EUDR could have significant implications. The regulation may impose stricter requirements on the sourcing and traceability of timber products, potentially increasing compliance costs and affecting market access. Additionally, there is concern that the regulation could lead to extensive interventions in the sustainable management of forests, agriculture, and grasslands in Germany, as highlighted by the campaign #LandschafftZukunft!, which warns of the massive consequences for food security and the establishment of climate-resilient forests if the regulation is not fundamentally revised.

Moreover, there is ongoing pressure from the US government on Germany and the EU to allow special provisions for the USA within the EUDR, which could further complicate the regulatory landscape for German timber exporters.

Are Wood Pellets Worth Billions In Subsidies? Drax Faces A Reckoning
ByKen Silverstein,Senior Contributor. Ken Silverstein covers global energy and climate issues.

Views Of Drax Power Station In North Yorkshire
SELBY, ENGLAND - JANUARY 30: The winter sun sets behind the Drax Bio mass Power Station operated by Drax Group PLC on January 30, 2025 in Selby, England. Once one of the largest coal power plants in Europe, the Drax power station in North Yorkshire now generates electricity by burning wood pellets sourced from trees in North America. (Photo by Christopher Furlong/Getty Images)

The energy industry initially sold wood pellets as a way to clean up coal. And governments bought into that assumption. But now policymakers are questioning that position and even reversing course.

At the center of this debate is a UK-based power company called Drax, which converted EuropeÆs largest coal plant into a biomass facilityŚone fueled by wood pellets that it imported from southern states in the United States. The debate raises a multitude of questions, namely those centered on pollution and costs. That is, if the additive creates more pollution than either wind or solar energy, why bother, especially since it comes from a power source that depends on subsidies?

Merry Dickinson, lead campaigner for the Stop Burning Trees Coalition in the U.K., told me that Drax now operates entirely on woody biomass, requiring seven to eight million tons of wood pellets annually. ōThe amount of wood required to run at these levels is beyond what is available as waste wood,ö Dickinson says. ōEven when the industry refers to æwaste wood,Æ much of the supply consists of whole trees.ö

Biomass received special treatment under the 2010 European Renewable Energy Directive, which counted emissions from burning wood as zero at the smokestack. The underlying assumption: trees regrow and reabsorb carbon over time. Post-Brexit, the UK expanded subsidies for biomass, positioning wood pellets as a bridge fuel to reduce reliance on coal. Under these rules, a power plant can burn wood and claim carbon neutralityŚa calculation not applied to fossil fuels.

DraxÆs business model has long relied on government incentives rather than market demand. The UK has historically provided about $1.1 billion annually in subsidies, but these payments are scheduled to be cut nearly in half after 2027. Meanwhile, Drax has shuttered facilities in Arkansas and British Columbia and scaled back investments in bioenergy with carbon capture and storage. Some operations are pivoting toward data centers, a quiet acknowledgment that the market cannot sustain the company at previous levels.

Unlike wind and solar, which generate electricity without combustion emissions, biomass combines emissions from burning wood chips with those from processing, transport, and ongoing fuel extraction. Subsidies for wind and solar have been declining per megawatt-hour as technology costs fall, while biomass continues to benefit from unusually high public support.

In a subsequent written statement, Drax said that the places from which it harvests wood are strictly regulated, with much of it coming from ōtree thinning," which is necessary to maintain the health of the forests. Moreover, it noted that ōBiomass, in the form of wood pellets, has been quietly making a big impact by helping countries reduce carbon emissions, support sustainable forestry, and transition away from fossil fuels. Scientists challenge the notion that wood pellets are worse than coal; this idea relies on unrealistic scenarios of forest management and harvesting.ö

Research Challenges Carbon Neutrality

Recent lifecycle analyses, including studies by MIT and Chatham House, dispute this and suggest the carbon math behind national policies promoting the mixing of wood pellets with coal is deeply flawed. Those studies have found that replacing coal with wood pellets can increase near-term carbon emissions, particularly when whole treesŚrather than waste residuesŚare used.

The process begins with harvesting mature trees, converting them into pellets through energy-intensive drying, shredding, and pressing, and shipping them overseas. Carbon stored in standing trees is released immediately, and regrowth can take decades to offset the emissions. Compounding the problem, wood has lower energy density than coal, meaning more volume must be burned to produce the same amount of electricity.

ōItÆs better to keep the trees on the land and keep all that carbon out of the atmosphere,ö said John Sterman, professor of management at MIT Sloan School of Management, in a 2018 release. ōA molecule of CO2 emitted today has the same impact on the climate whether it comes from coal or biomass. Declaring that biofuels are carbon neutral, as the EU, UK, and others have done, erroneously assumes that forest regrowth happens quickly and fully offsets the emissions from biofuel production and combustion.ö

In Mississippi, residents have sued Drax over alleged air pollution violations at its Gloster pellet millŚa case that highlights the local costs of an energy source often discussed only in global carbon terms. The plant uses cranes to move whole trees, suggesting a reliance on raw, virgin wood and not waste wood.

Meanwhile, regulators have previously fined the plant for violations involving volatile organic compounds, methanol, and formaldehyde. The plantÆs operations highlight that biomass power not only has carbon implications but also presents tangible local air quality risks.

ōForest-derived biomass is the focus,ö says Michķl Legendre, campaign director for the Dogwood Alliance, in an interview. ōDraxÆs operations require immense amounts of trees, clear-cutting acres every yearŚnot just twigs or leftovers.ö

Adding wood pellets to the combustion process was well-intentionedŚto clean coal, which was then the leading method of electricity generation. However, since the processÆs introduction, many things have changed, including the sharp drop in wind and solar prices and new studies showing that the pellets did not fulfill their promises. As a result, regulators are rightfully reviewing their policiesŚones that focus on truly sustainable sources of power.

Query: How do European Central Bank interest rate decisions affect the German construction market?
Response:
The European Central Bank's (ECB) interest rate decisions significantly impact the German construction market. By maintaining the key interest rate at 2.0%, as announced in their recent meeting, the ECB aims to manage inflation risks, particularly those arising from energy costs.

This decision influences borrowing costs for construction projects. For instance, the average interest rates for 10-year loans have recently risen to nearly 3.8%. Higher borrowing costs can lead to reduced investment in construction, as financing becomes more expensive for developers and builders. Additionally, the yields on 10-year German government bonds serve as a key indicator for real estate loans, further linking ECB policies to the construction market dynamics.

Overall, the ECB's stance on interest rates can create volatility in the financing landscape, affecting both the availability of credit and the overall economic environment for construction activities in Germany.

Query: What is the significance of carbon certificates for the German biomass and timber industry?
Response:
The significance of carbon certificates for the German biomass and timber industry lies in their potential to create new growth opportunities and enhance sustainability efforts. As policymakers are rethinking the role of biomass, particularly wood pellets, in carbon emissions, the focus is shifting towards subsidies and innovative uses of biomass.

Research indicates that biomass can be utilized as a raw material for chemical products instead of relying solely on petroleum, which could lead to more sustainable practices in the industry. Additionally, the mention of technologies like BECCS (Bioenergy with Carbon Capture and Storage) suggests that carbon certificates could play a crucial role in incentivizing the reduction of emissions and promoting negative emissions technologies.

Moreover, the example of the Fujian Yongan Forestry company highlights the potential for monetizing carbon certificates as a way to explore new growth areas in a stabilizing Chinese wood market. This indicates that carbon certificates could be a valuable asset for companies in the German biomass and timber industry as they navigate the evolving market landscape and seek to align with environmental goals.

Are Wood Pellets Worth Billions In Subsidies? Drax Faces A Reckoning: The energy industry initially sold wood pellets as a way to clean up coal. And governments bought into that assumption. But now policymakers are questioning that position and even reversing course.

At the center of this debate is a UK-based power company called Drax, which converted EuropeÆs largest coal plant into a biomass facilityŚone fueled by wood pellets that it imported from southern states in the United States. The debate raises a multitude of questions, namely those centered on pollution and costs. That is, if the additive creates more pollution than either wind or solar energy, why bother, especially since it comes from a power source that depends on subsidies?

Merry Dickinson, lead campaigner for the Stop Burning Trees Coalition in the U.K., told me that Drax now operates entirely on woody biomass, requiring seven to eight million tons of wood pellets annually. ōThe amount of wood required to run at these levels is beyond what is available as waste wood,ö Dickinson says. ōEven when the industry refers to æwaste wood,Æ much of the supply consists of whole trees.ö

Biomass received special treatment under the 2010 European Renewable Energy Directive, which counted emissions from burning wood as zero at the smokestack. The underlying assumption: trees regrow and reabsorb carbon over time. Post-Brexit, the UK expanded subsidies for biomass, positioning wood pellets as a bridge fuel to reduce reliance on coal. Under these rules, a power plant can burn wood and claim carbon neutralityŚa calculation not applied to fossil fuels.

DraxÆs business model has long relied on government incentives rather than market demand. The UK has historically provided about $1.1 billion annually in subsidies, but these payments are scheduled to be cut nearly in half after 2027. Meanwhile, Drax has shuttered facilities in Arkansas and British Columbia and scaled back investments in bioenergy with carbon capture and storage. Some operations are pivoting toward data centers, a quiet acknowledgment that the market cannot sustain the company at previous levels.

Biomass-derived furans offer sustainable alternative to petroleum in chemical production: A research project conducted by the Max-Planck-Institut f³r Kohlenforschung shows how biomass can be used as a raw material for chemical products instead of petroleum. The scientists have published their findings in the journal Science.The chemical industry is facing major challenges: for reasons of CO2 neutrality, circular economy, and geopolitical instability, there is a desire to move away from petroleum and other fossil materials as raw materials for the production of high-quality chemicals. But how will molecular building blocks for essential medicines, for example, be obtained in the future?

New approach to using biomass
A team from the working group of Prof. Benjamin List, Director at the Max-Planck-Institut f³r Kohlenforschung, is now demonstrating a new way to obtain such chemicals.

"Our motto is: biomass as a chemical raw material instead of petroleum," explains Nils Frank, a doctoral student in Benjamin List's team. But unlike petroleum, the chemical potential of biomass is far from being exhausted. In their project, Nils Frank and the team from the List laboratory focused on so-called furans, a product of biomass.

"For decades, chemists have been focusing on petroleum, which is why the possibilities offered by furans have hardly been investigated. We took a closer look at this," says Nils Frank.

Breakthrough in furan chemistry
Established processes oxidize or reduce furans to alcohols or carboxylic acidsŚa simple, redox-neutral ring opening to, for example, the dialdehyde succinaldehyde was not known. This redox-neutral reaction was achieved thanks to a process known as photohydrolysis.

"Light is important because the reaction is an 'uphill' reaction," explains Nils Frank. This means that energy must be supplied for the reaction to take place. And, as in natural photosynthesis, this energy comes from light.

"Carbon dioxide and light are the building blocks of a future chemical industry, and Nils' discovery is just the beginning of our work in this direction," says Ben List.

Biomass could play a key role in Canada's transition to a carbon-neutral economy: Record forest fires, under-utilized agricultural residues like straw and husks and struggling sawmills have left Canada with an abundance of undervalued biomass. If carefully and strategically managed, this resource could become a powerful ally in the fight against climate change.Canada's biomass sectors are facing significant uncertainty because of political and natural disruptions. The forestry sector was hit last year by new American tariffs announced by the Donald Trump administration on Canadian forest products, bringing the total duties imposed on Canadian lumber to 45%.

The agricultural and agri-food sector is also particularly vulnerable, since it exports more than 70% of its main crops.

In addition to facing these political uncertainties, biomass sectors are increasingly experiencing the effects of climate disasters. In 2025, fires had burned 8.3 million hectares of Canadian forests by Sept. 30, making it Canada's second-worst wildfire season on record. With climate change, extreme weather events like wildfires and droughts are likely to become more frequent and intense.

Change is accelerating and risks are mounting. For industries and communities that rely on biomass, this is the moment to define a long-term role in the climate transition.Biomass resources
Canada needs to move toward a carbon-neutral economy, and the biomass sectors have a key role to play in this transition.

The availability of diverse biomass resources in Canada's forests and agricultural lands, combined with new technologies to convert them into bioproducts and bioenergy, makes biomass a potential solution for reducing carbon emissions in several sectors, including industry, construction and all modes of transport (road, marine, rail and air).

Biomass can be part of climate change mitigation strategies. Used properly, it can replace fossil fuels and products, and help store carbon in different ways: in sustainable materials made from wood or agricultural residues, in the form of biochar that traps carbon in the soil or through bioenergy with carbon capture and storage (BECCS), which prevents carbon released during energy production from entering the atmosphere.

Several recent projects have demonstrated that interest in biomass feedstocks is high in many industries. In 2025, Canada's first industrial-scale biochar plant was inaugurated in Quķbec, while the Strathcona refinery in Alberta will become Canada's largest facility for renewable diesel.

The potential role of biomass becomes clear in the pathways now being modeled to achieve Canada's climate goals. These analyses show that if a significant portion of available biomass were used differently, it would be possible to sequester up to 94 million tons of CO? equivalent per year through BECCS and biochar.

These results underscore the need for Canada to carefully plan new project developments and judiciously allocate biomass between its traditional and emerging uses.

Query: How do rising European Central Bank interest rates affect German housing construction demand and in turn timber consumption?
Response:
Rising European Central Bank (ECB) interest rates are expected to have a negative impact on housing construction demand in Germany. The current situation indicates that construction costs and the willingness to pay are hindering residential building projects. As interest rates for 10-year loans rise, currently averaging nearly 3.8 percent, borrowing costs for potential homeowners and developers increase. This can lead to a decrease in housing demand, which in turn may reduce timber consumption, as less construction activity typically results in lower demand for timber products.

Additionally, the construction industry is facing pressures from rising energy prices, which could further increase building material costs and affect overall construction activity. Therefore, the combination of higher interest rates and escalating energy costs may lead to a slowdown in the housing market, subsequently impacting timber consumption in Germany.

Query: How does bark beetle damage in German forests affect sawmill production and ultimately timber prices?
Response:
The bark beetle damage in German forests has significant implications for sawmill production and timber prices. As reported, the bark beetle has already destroyed 16,000 hectares of forest in the region, leading to a substantial reduction in the availability of healthy timber. This reduction in timber supply can create a scarcity in the market, which may drive up timber prices as demand remains steady or increases.

Additionally, the ongoing forest damage from bark beetles, along with other factors such as wildfires and storms, is expected to worsen in the coming years. This anticipated increase in forest damage could further exacerbate the supply issues faced by sawmills, potentially leading to higher production costs and reduced output. Consequently, these factors combined could contribute to an upward pressure on timber prices in the market.

Overall, the bark beetle damage poses a challenge for the timber industry, affecting both the availability of raw materials for sawmills and the pricing dynamics in the timber market.
context_1:
Even if warming is limited to 2░C, wildfires, storms and beetles may boost Europe forest loss. Forest damage in Europe caused by wildfires, storms and bark beetle outbreaks is projected to increase compared to recent decades under all analyzed climate scenarios, according to a new international study, published in Science, with contributions from the Potsdam Institute for Climate Impact Research (PIK).In a scenario in which fossil fuel use continues to increase, annually disturbed forest area could double, reaching nearly 370,000 hectares per year by the end of the century.

"In the future, Europe's forests are likely to absorb less carbon," says Christopher Reyer, scientist at PIK and co-author of the study.

"If forests take up less carbon, or potentially even release more than they absorb, this increases pressure on other sectors such as transport and agriculture to reduce their emissions more rapidly. At the same time, forest management needs to focus more strongly on building resilient forests."

According to the study, forests in Southern and Western Europe will be particularly affected and will undergo the strongest changes in forest disturbance. Northern Europe is expected to be less severely impacted overall, though hotspots of future forest damage are also likely to emerge there. The study was led by researchers at the Technical University of Munich (TUM).

Are Wood Pellets Worth Billions In Subsidies? Drax Faces A Reckoning: The energy industry initially sold wood pellets as a way to clean up coal. And governments bought into that assumption. But now policymakers are questioning that position and even reversing course.

At the center of this debate is a UK-based power company called Drax, which converted EuropeÆs largest coal plant into a biomass facilityŚone fueled by wood pellets that it imported from southern states in the United States. The debate raises a multitude of questions, namely those centered on pollution and costs. That is, if the additive creates more pollution than either wind or solar energy, why bother, especially since it comes from a power source that depends on subsidies?

Merry Dickinson, lead campaigner for the Stop Burning Trees Coalition in the U.K., told me that Drax now operates entirely on woody biomass, requiring seven to eight million tons of wood pellets annually. ōThe amount of wood required to run at these levels is beyond what is available as waste wood,ö Dickinson says. ōEven when the industry refers to æwaste wood,Æ much of the supply consists of whole trees.ö

Biomass received special treatment under the 2010 European Renewable Energy Directive, which counted emissions from burning wood as zero at the smokestack. The underlying assumption: trees regrow and reabsorb carbon over time. Post-Brexit, the UK expanded subsidies for biomass, positioning wood pellets as a bridge fuel to reduce reliance on coal. Under these rules, a power plant can burn wood and claim carbon neutralityŚa calculation not applied to fossil fuels.

DraxÆs business model has long relied on government incentives rather than market demand. The UK has historically provided about $1.1 billion annually in subsidies, but these payments are scheduled to be cut nearly in half after 2027. Meanwhile, Drax has shuttered facilities in Arkansas and British Columbia and scaled back investments in bioenergy with carbon capture and storage. Some operations are pivoting toward data centers, a quiet acknowledgment that the market cannot sustain the company at previous levels.
